primary_detection.name: Single Character Variables Throughout
primary_detection.description: Entire codebase uses single letter variables to obscure functionality
evidence: a = __import__ - Single char for import function
b = 'os' - Single char for OS module name
c = a(b) - Single char for imported module
d = c.system - Single char for system function
Function parameters also single chars (p, q, r, etc.)

underlying_threats.name: Home Directory Exfiltration
underlying_threats.description: Reads .bashrc, .ssh, and .aws directories and sends externally

underlying_threats.name: Dynamic Import Obfuscation
underlying_threats.description: Uses __import__ indirection to hide module usage

underlying_threats.name: HTTP POST Exfiltration
underlying_threats.description: Uses requests.post to send data to external server

underlying_threats.name: Environment Variable Access
underlying_threats.description: Reads HOME and HOSTNAME for reconnaissance

underlying_threats.name: Auto-Execution on Import
underlying_threats.description: Function u() called at module load time

obfuscation_techniques.purpose: Maximum obfuscation with minimal character usage

verdict_reasoning: Systematic use of single-character variables throughout entire codebase is a classic malware obfuscation technique. Combined with credential theft targeting SSH keys and AWS credentials, this is clearly malicious.
recommended_actions: Flag any code with >80% single-char variables as suspicious
